refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion (acceptance gate) dependent on operational execution, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—to provide visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met during field operations. The target achievement is an acceptance criterion evaluated post-deployment, not a design requirement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) with defined accuracy and latency during representative operations. Verify data logging, display, and export mechanisms. Acceptance of the target value itself is a separate operational validation gate, not a functional verification of this FR.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) from operational sensors.
FR-NGHE-001.2: Calculate and derive throughput metrics (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired data.
FR-NGHE-001.3: Display current and historical throughput metrics to authorized users in real-time or near-real-time.
FR-NGHE-001.4: Log and store throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input or output) over time and derive throughput from level-delta and cross-sectional geometry
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous flow sensing) with reconciliation logic to validate throughput estimate
DP5: Instrument material handling equipment (conveyor, pump, loader) with operational state and load telemetry, then infer throughput from duty cycle and nominal capacity
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor placement and material stream geometry: continuous sensing requires stable, representative access to the flow; poor placement introduces systematic bias and couples measurement accuracy to site layout

Calibration and environmental drift: continuous sensors (density, velocity, mass flow) are sensitive to temperature, moisture, and material property variation; calibration must be decoupled from operational state to avoid false coupling

Integration and time-synchronization: cumulative throughput depends on accurate time-stamping and numerical integration; clock drift or asynchronous data collection introduces coupling between measurement and reporting intervals

Acceptance criteria coupling: the requirement that reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over an evaluation period creates implicit coupling between measurement accuracy and the target threshold; systematic bias in sensing directly affects pass/fail outcome
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is logically sound and supports independence of measurement from material type (within sensor range) and from downstream reporting. However, realization (IM layer) will introduce coupling through sensor placement, calibration procedures, and data pipeline synchronization. These are manageable if architectural boundaries (sensor module, calibration module, integration module) are clearly owned and their interfaces are well-defined.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material stream is accessible and stable enough for continuous sensing without disruption to operations. Risk: Abrasive, sticky, or highly variable materials may degrade sensor performance or require frequent maintenance, reducing measurement availability.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough (hours to days) that integration error and sensor drift are small relative to cumulative throughput. Risk: Short evaluation windows (< 1 hour) amplify relative error; acceptance criteria may be unachievable if productivity_target is set without accounting for measurement uncertainty.

Assumption: Environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, pressure) remain within sensor specification during evaluation. Risk: Outdoor or uncontrolled worksites may violate this; sensor drift or failure could invalidate results.

Assumption: Instantaneous flow rate can be inferred from a single measurement modality (e.g., velocity × cross-section, or mass flow sensor output). Risk: If material properties (density, particle size distribution) vary significantly, a single modality may not be representative; DP4 (multi-modal) may be necessary.

Assumption: Cumulative throughput is the appropriate metric for acceptance. Risk: If productivity_target is defined as instantaneous rate (e.g., tons/hour at peak), integration over a longer period may mask transient underperformance; acceptance criteria must align with DP.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of continuous sensing equipment without additional hazard mitigation. Risk: Electrical, mechanical, or chemical hazards may require sensor enclosure, isolation, or redundancy, increasing IM complexity.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) whether throughput is cumulative (total material over evaluation period) or instantaneous (rate at a point in time); (2) the expected range of material properties (density, particle size, moisture) to bound sensor selection; (3) the evaluation period duration and acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% of productivity_target); (4) environmental operating conditions (temperature, humidity, outdoor/indoor). DP1 is well-suited for cumulative throughput measurement over extended periods (≥ 4 hours) with stable material properties. If instantaneous rate or short-window acceptance is required, DP2 (batch counting) or DP4 (multi-modal) should be reconsidered. At the IM layer, establish clear ownership boundaries: (a) sensor acquisition and calibration module, (b) data pipeline and time-synchronization module, (c) integration and reporting module. Define interfaces (data format, sampling rate, calibration schedule) to decouple these modules and enable independent testing and maintenance.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and cumulative throughput computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) via telemetry channel

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives from external tasking authority

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses network link for bidirectional communication of sensor data and control signals if remote sensing or distributed computation is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: continuous sensing hardware, signal acquisition, integration logic, and cumulative computation are all onboard vehicle systems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry output and tasking input cross boundary; core DP logic remains encapsulated.

Poor containment: NGHE Solution is a high-level orchestration/planning layer, not a sensing platform. Flow rate acquisition hardware and real-time integration would need to diffuse to Vehicle anyway. Computation could theoretically occur here but would require continuous telemetry streaming and introduce latency.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. Sensing hardware, signal conditioning, and real-time integration belong on the Vehicle. Placing DP logic here would create tight coupling to Vehicle sensors and introduce unacceptable latency for continuous flow measurement.

Very poor containment: Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard monitoring and alert generation. Flow rate measurement is a functional capability, not a safety function. Placing DP here violates separation of concerns and would diffuse sensing/integration logic across safety and operational domains.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because it is the physical platform where the material stream is present, where sensing hardware is mounted, where real-time signal acquisition occurs, and where integration computation can execute with minimal latency. The DP is fully contained within Vehicle's scope; external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link) are minimal and well-defined.
containment_risks: If Vehicle lacks onboard computational capacity, integration logic may need to be offloaded to NGHE Solution, creating a split DP realization and increasing coupling.

If sensor calibration or evaluation period parameters are frequently updated by external systems, Tasking interface coupling may increase; mitigation is to cache parameters onboard and update asynchronously.

If multiple external consumers require flow rate data at different update rates or formats, Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck; mitigation is to define a single canonical telemetry schema and let consumers filter/resample.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable via the Vehicle's onboard integration logic and telemetry output. The evaluation period is a tasking parameter, enabling repeatable measurement cycles. Worksite safety plan constraints are external governance; flow measurement itself does not introduce new safety hazards if sensor hardware is properly mounted and signal conditioning is isolated.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Vehicle IM boundary is unambiguous: it owns all hardware sensing, signal conditioning, real-time acquisition, and cumulative integration. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link bidirectional) are minimal and well-scoped. Traceability from FR (measure throughput) → DP (continuous sensing + integration) → IM (Vehicle onboard systems) is direct and verifiable.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 is logically sound and complete. Continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) with integration over the evaluation period is a standard and proven approach for throughput measurement. The DP directly addresses the FR without ambiguity. No functional gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Computational offload risk: If Vehicle lacks sufficient onboard compute capacity for real-time integration, integration logic may diffuse to NGHE Solution, creating a split DP realization across Vehicle (sensing) and NGHE (computation). Mitigation: Confirm Vehicle has adequate CPU/memory for integration; if not, redesign DP to use edge-cloud hybrid with clear ownership of each stage.

Sensor calibration coupling: Tasking interface for calibration commands introduces external dependency. If calibration parameters change frequently or are pushed reactively, Vehicle must handle asynchronous updates without losing measurement continuity. Mitigation: Cache calibration parameters onboard; define update protocol (e.g., apply only between evaluation periods).

Telemetry bottleneck: If multiple external consumers (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services, external dashboards) request flow rate data at different rates or formats, Telemetry interface may become a diffusion point for data transformation logic. Mitigation: Define single canonical telemetry schema; consumers filter/resample locally.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Core DP logic (continuous sensing, signal conditioning, integration) is fully encapsulated within Vehicle. Diffusion is minimal (0.08 score) and limited to well-defined external interfaces. Alternative modules (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are correctly identified as poor candidates due to lack of sensing hardware, real-time capability, or functional relevance. No architectural packaging defects detected.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly document Vehicle's onboard computational capacity (CPU, memory, real-time OS guarantees) to confirm integration logic can execute without offload. If capacity is marginal, define fallback DP variant (e.g., streaming raw sensor data to NGHE for post-hoc integration) and acceptance criteria for each variant.

Define Tasking interface protocol for sensor calibration and evaluation period updates: specify whether updates are applied immediately, cached until next evaluation cycle, or require Vehicle acknowledgment. Document latency and consistency guarantees.

Define Telemetry schema (instantaneous flow rate, cumulative throughput, timestamp, sensor status, evaluation period metadata) and specify update frequency. If multiple consumers exist, document filtering/resampling responsibilities to prevent diffusion of data transformation logic into Vehicle.

Add verification plan: specify how productivity_target will be validated during representative operations (e.g., ground truth measurement method, acceptable measurement error bounds, evaluation period duration). Confirm worksite safety plan does not impose additional constraints on sensor placement or signal routing.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92). However, the recommended_revisions identify three critical dependencies that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition: (1) Vehicle onboard computational capacity confirmation—a foundational assumption for DP feasibility; (2) Tasking interface protocol for calibration and evaluation period management—essential for DP correctness under operational variability; (3) Telemetry schema and consumer coordination—necessary to prevent diffusion of data transformation logic. Additionally, safety criticality (high) requires explicit verification planning to confirm that sensor placement and signal routing do not introduce new hazards or violate worksite safety constraints. These are not architectural defects but rather unresolved design assumptions that, if left unaddressed, will propagate ambiguity and risk into lower-level decomposition. Revising the current level to document these assumptions and define acceptance criteria will strengthen the foundation for sub-FR derivation.

revise.why: DP1 (continuous sensing + integration) is logically sound but relies on three unvalidated assumptions: (1) Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute capacity for real-time integration without offload; (2) Tasking interface can reliably deliver calibration and evaluation period updates with defined latency and consistency guarantees; (3) Telemetry interface can serve multiple external consumers without diffusing data transformation logic into Vehicle. Revise DP to explicitly state these assumptions as design constraints and define fallback variants (e.g., edge-cloud hybrid integration, cached calibration parameters, canonical telemetry schema). Additionally, add a verification sub-DP: 'Validate throughput measurement accuracy against ground truth method during representative operations, with acceptable error bounds and evaluation period duration specified.' This sub-DP addresses the high safety criticality by ensuring measurement reliability before operational deployment.

assumptions: Vehicle onboard compute platform (CPU, memory, real-time OS) has sufficient capacity to execute continuous sensing, signal conditioning, and cumulative integration logic without offloading to NGHE Solution or external edge compute.

Tasking interface can deliver sensor calibration parameters and evaluation period updates with latency ≤ [TBD] and consistency guarantees (e.g., no loss of updates, atomic application between evaluation cycles).

Telemetry interface can support multiple external consumers (NGHE, Teleoperation, Safety Services, external dashboards) with a single canonical schema; consumers are responsible for local filtering and resampling.
Worksite safety plan does not impose constraints on sensor placement, signal routing, or electrical isolation that would conflict with onboard sensing hardware integration.
Ground truth measurement method exists (e.g., independent flow meter, manual tally, calibrated reference) to validate throughput measurement accuracy during representative operations.
Acceptable measurement error bounds (e.g., ±5% of productivity_target) and evaluation period duration (e.g., 1 shift, 1 week) are defined and agreed with stakeholders.
open_risks: Computational offload risk: If Vehicle lacks onboard compute capacity, integration logic will diffuse to NGHE Solution, creating split DP realization and complicating real-time guarantees. Mitigation: Confirm Vehicle CPU/memory specifications; if marginal, define edge-cloud hybrid DP variant with clear ownership boundaries.

Sensor calibration coupling: Tasking interface for calibration updates introduces external dependency. If updates are asynchronous or frequent, Vehicle must handle parameter changes without losing measurement continuity or introducing transient errors. Mitigation: Define update protocol (e.g., cache parameters, apply only between evaluation cycles, require acknowledgment).

Telemetry bottleneck: Multiple external consumers requesting flow rate data at different rates or formats may diffuse data transformation logic into Vehicle. Mitigation: Define single canonical telemetry schema; enforce consumer-side filtering/resampling.

Safety criticality validation gap: High safety criticality requires explicit verification that sensor placement, signal routing, and integration logic do not introduce new hazards (e.g., electrical shock, signal interference, false positives/negatives affecting operator decisions). Mitigation: Add verification sub-DP with ground truth validation and error bound acceptance criteria.

Evaluation period variability: If evaluation period is tasking-driven (e.g., per-shift, per-job), Vehicle must handle period boundaries correctly (e.g., reset cumulative counters, timestamp transitions). Mitigation: Define evaluation period state machine and boundary handling protocol.
evidence_needed: Vehicle onboard platform specification: CPU model, memory (RAM, storage), real-time OS (RTOS) type, real-time guarantees (e.g., interrupt latency, task scheduling determinism), available I/O bandwidth for sensor integration.

Tasking interface protocol specification: message format, update frequency, latency bounds, consistency guarantees (e.g., exactly-once delivery, atomic application), error handling (e.g., stale parameter detection, rollback).

Telemetry schema definition: data fields (instantaneous flow rate, cumulative throughput, timestamp, sensor status, evaluation period metadata, error flags), update frequency, consumer list, filtering/resampling responsibilities.

Worksite safety plan review: constraints on sensor placement (e.g., clearance from operator zones, cable routing), electrical isolation requirements, hazard analysis for sensing hardware (e.g., shock risk, EMI/RFI susceptibility).

Ground truth measurement method: description of independent measurement approach (e.g., reference sensor, manual tally, calibrated scale), accuracy specification, operational conditions (e.g., material type, flow rate range, environmental factors).

Verification plan: acceptance criteria for throughput measurement accuracy (e.g., error bounds ±5%), evaluation period duration (e.g., 1 shift, 1 week), number of test cycles, representative operational scenarios (e.g., nominal flow, peak flow, material variation).

Sensor hardware datasheets: specifications for mass/volume flow sensors (e.g., measurement range, accuracy, response time, calibration interval, environmental tolerance), signal conditioning electronics (e.g., noise floor, linearity, temperature drift).
